answers: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - short - Most likely cause: a Windows update + VM snapshot/restore sequence broke the SAP -> Windows print‑queue exposure (printer network‑share publishing or queue registration) or created a driver/OS mismatch on the two SAP print‑server VMs. The outage pattern you describe (Office/follow‑me printing OK; SAP prints stuck) plus the recorded restore after maintenance fits that root cause [Data: Sources (28, 29); Reports (16, 102)]. - Important note: blue‑screens (BSODs) were observed on the restored VMs in the incident timeline, so treat this as an OS/driver/hypervisor issue that needs forensic capture before broad changes [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. Most likely failure modes (ranked) 1. Printer network‑share / AD publishing or SMB exposure was corrupted by the restore (SAP jobs can't see the published queues though Office printing paths still work). Toggling the printer share is a known quick remediation in this scenario [Data: Reports (102, 28)]. 2. A driver or Windows update regression (print driver, kernel/SMB, or integration component) introduced an incompatibility; restore/rollback created a mismatch between guest drivers and the host or new OS modules and the queue‑publishing components [Data: Sources (28); Reports (16, 173)]. 3. Snapshot/restore inconsistency (registry/queue metadata, SMB bindings, or guest‑agent state not re‑published after restore), or hypervisor/host/VM‑tools mismatch revealed by the snapshot restore [Data: Sources (29); Reports (100, 173)]. What to check and do first (do these in order) 1. Stop - do not rollback production Windows yet. Preserve evidence and snapshots. The incident guidance is explicit: collect logs/dumps and preserve the snapshot used for restore before any rollback [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. 2. Confirm SAP‑to‑Windows handoff: - In SAP: check the spool requests (SP01) and note the SAP Print Target names and timestamps. This determines whether jobs leave SAP. - On the Windows printserver(s): check if those same jobs arrive in the Windows queue (Get-PrintJob / Print Management). If jobs appear on the Windows queue but never print, the fault is post‑handoff (printserver/share/driver) [Data: Sources (7); Reports (28)]. 3. Verify printer share publication and network exposure (quick, low‑risk): - Run Get-Printer and Get-SmbShare (or net share) on VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 and confirm the expected queues are Shared and Published. If the share is missing or not published, a controlled disable->enable of the printer network share often clears stuck SAP jobs (this was the remediation recorded in the outage) - but capture logs first [Data: Reports (102, 28)]. 4. Collect immediate artifacts (forensics) before further changes: - Export System and PrintService (Microsoft‑Windows‑PrintService/Operational) event logs for the restore/BSOD window. - Copy C:\Windows\Minidump\*.dmp and MEMORY.DMP if present. - Export Get-HotFix, driver list (Get-PrinterDriver or driverquery / pnputil), and Get-Printer output. These artifacts are required because BSODs were observed after the restore and are essential to safe remediation [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16, 173)]. 5. Compare drivers/versions: - Export Get-PrinterDriver (Name, Version, InfName) from the two affected SAP printservers and from a known‑good follow‑me printserver and diff them. Look for driver version changes or unsigned drivers that could explain the regression [Data: Sources (28); Reports (173)]. 6. Check Windows Update history and recent patches: - Get‑HotFix / DISM package list to identify which KBs were applied prior to the snapshot used and correlate them with the failure window. Do not assume a particular KB - confirm from the server/patch system first [Data: Sources (28); Reports (173)]. 7. If BSODs present: preserve VM snapshot and export VM memory (hypervisor level) if the VM will not produce a reliable guest dump. Analyse minidumps with WinDbg (or hand to platform team) to identify faulting module/driver. This is required because kernel crashes point at drivers or host/guest compatibility issues [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. Concrete quick checks / commands to run (examples) - On Windows printservers (run elevated): - Get-Printer | Format-Table Name,Shared,ShareName,Published - Get-SmbShare | Format-Table Name,Path,ScopeName - Get-PrintJob -PrinterName "<queue>" - Get-PrinterDriver | Select Name,Manufacturer,DriverVersion,InfName | Export-Csv C:\Temp\printerDrivers.csv - Get-HotFix | Sort InstalledOn | Export-Csv C:\Temp\hotfixes.csv - wevtutil epl System C:\Temp\System.evtx - wevtutil epl "Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational" C:\Temp\PrintService.evtx (If you want, I can provide a one‑click PowerShell collection script to export these.) These are the same categories of artifacts recommended by the incident runbook [Data: Reports (16, 100)]. Short remediation path (safe / recommended) - If the Windows queue shows SAP jobs but they are stuck: capture logs, then try a controlled disable->enable of the printer share for one queue (document timestamps and collect logs before/after). The recorded outage showed this action cleared the backlog quickly [Data: Reports (102, 28)]. - If driver/version mismatch is identified: reproduce on a clone and test driver rollback (or install the vendor-recommended driver) on the test clone first - do not perform blind production rollbacks [Data: Reports (173, 16)]. - If a specific Windows KB is implicated by hotfix timestamps and dump analysis, do a staged rollback on a clone and validate end‑to‑end SAP test prints before any production rollback. Preserve snapshots and evidence always [Data: Sources (28); Reports (16, 173)]. Ticket classification and ownership - Classify this as a 2nd‑level (infrastructure/platform) ticket - the failure surface is OS/driver/VM/hypervisor and requires server/virtualization expertise and forensic analysis (spooler restarts and reboots already did not resolve the issue) [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. First‑line can do the initial SAP spool check and collect basic evidence, but escalate quickly to Infrastructure/Platform for driver, crash‑dump and snapshot analysis. What to tell users now (short) - Tell stakeholders you will not do an immediate production rollback. You will (a) preserve the snapshot(s) and collect crash dumps and event logs, (b) attempt a low‑risk share toggle in a controlled manner after capturing logs if jobs already appear on the Windows queue, and (c) reproduce any rollback on a cloned VM before considering a production rollback. This prevents destroying forensic evidence and repeating BSODs seen previously [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16, 173)]. If you want - I can paste a short PowerShell evidence‑collection script you can run on a printserver (exports Get-PrinterDriver, Get-HotFix, event logs, and minidump copy) and a one‑line hypervisor export request to send to the virtualization team to preserve the snapshot and export VM memory for analysis.
